replies.text: I am offended that so many people are suckers that fall for Davidsons BS.  Anyone can fake Tourette's and spend decades getting away with ruining peoples special moments with N and F words.  They manipulate people's emotions and use their emotions against them in disgusting ways.  Jeremy literally hates on WOKE LGBT freaks doing the same exact thing.  But, apparently, if a clown fakes a syndrome, he gets a free pass to be an ass.  None of this adds up.  Did Jeremy become ignotarded and a sucker because it suited his narrative for some reason?  People with an extensive history of "Tourette's", be it authentic or fake, are compelled by everyone around them, including family, to not be placed in positions that would expose them, or others, to humiliation.  People with issues have an obligation to not cause other people to suffer consequences.  Davidson dresses like an ass, so, what are the chances that he is an ass himself?  hmm,... more than likely.

replies.author: @Veteran_Nerd
replies.text:
@SanDiegosGreatest Here's a list of all the documentaries and movie they did about John over the years. This has been public for decades, goofy

Documentaries (real-life follow-ups):
- John's Not Mad (1989) – BBC Q.E.D. series; original iconic doc on teenage John.
- The Boy Can't Help It (2002) – BBC follow-up; revisits John at ~30, includes young Greg Storey.
- Tourette de France (2007) – Channel 4 (Only Human); John travels with Keith Allen and others with TS.
- Tourettes: I Swear I Can't Help It (2009) – BBC follow-up; revisits John (37) and Greg (15).
Later appearances (not dedicated full docs): 
- Tourettes & Me (2014, BBC) and Tourette’s: Teenage Tics (2016, BBC).

Feature film (dramatized biopic):I Swear (2025) – Starring Robert Aramayo as John; covers his life from 1980s diagnosis onward, inspired by the 1989 doc and his advocacy.

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@SanDiegosGreatest 
What the hell are you even talking about? Make *WHAT* known? Tourette's syndrome? It's been known since it was first described in detail in 1857. You seem to be telling people commenting here to prove that they have Tourette's by doing...something...in public. But that makes absolutely no sense, because no one here claimed to have Tourette's. And the man at the BAFTAs _has made it known publicly already._

Or are you demanding people here use the n-word in public? That's just as m0r0nic as the other option. 

Before you try interacting with humans online, you might want to make sure you aren't an id10t with an IQ smaller than your shoe size.

replies.author: @glerp10000000000
replies.text: This is the problem we have here, and everywhere. People expressing opinions about opinions about opinions. Opinions are not FACTS. Facts are facts. The facts are Guy with condition blurts out a word. Two actors ignore it and carry on. Then  people chime in with what they think other people should have done, or shouldn't have and then more people chime in with opinions about what they said. One could argue that Jordan had class by letting it go, and getting on with the job. Instead, everybody starts  judging people left right and centre, they should have done this, they should have done that, as though each person is an expert and should be consulted at all times on everything. All because everybody thinks they are always right.

replies.text:
You know those old jokes about blond chicks? Think of those embodied in an actual individual, but they are also a "professional" movie reviewer, and she uploads them to YouTube with the editing polish of a 2007 YouTube essay. 

At random and rare moments she manages to say something somewhat insightful, and it's always short-lived. Her takes are usually as bad as her self-awareness, case and point being her actually off-putting tendency during her reviews to shamelessly indulge in the female gaze towards actors whilst becoming absolutely livid whenever she perceives what she thinks is an example of the male gaze towards actresses. She's a lolcow that says some really stupid things about movies in an annoying voice that people tend to find really funny, and that's all she was known for until now.

author: @Bonesawisready926
text: Why should Davidson apologize for something that was completely out of his control? I checked out the trailer for the movie about him (actually doesn't look too bad) and he was saying stuff like "Spunk for milk" when the topic of tea was brought up. This guys tics are well known. At worst, the people who need to apologize are the BAFTAs for seating him so close to microphones and not disclosing he was there to the entire audience. The reaction of people like Randolph and Jamie Foxx actually kind of proves the point of the film based on his life. I seriously doubt Davidson is looking at videos about this, but on the off chance he is, you got nobody to apologize to mate.

replies.author: @jcorbett9620
replies.text: Aparently, according to Davidson, the ENTIRE audience was warned that there was someone in the room who might utter unintended phrases or words, that he was NOT responsible for, nor intended any offence by them. He said he received a round of applause and felt "very welcome". He didn't say if that was extended to the presenters, but you would think it was.

author: @redbumbabboon1
text:
It might sound weird for me to say but I DONT THINK THE WORD SHOULD BE CUT.

Hear me out.

It was not said in malice, it was said as part of an uncontrollable condition.

A word without intent behind it is just another noise.

I think that they would be better having an explanation on screen as to why someone shouted, to help raise awareness, just like what his film is about.

To me cutting the word, undermines his condition.  Could you imagine a person in a wheelchair going up for an award and the camera on purposely focusing on the top half of the body to hide their disability?

There would be outrage that you are trying to hide the disability of people if you did that.

This to me is no different.

author: @Jimmyduudah
text: This just shows that the overwhelming majority of people who claim they get “offended” by someone saying that word we cannot say are full of shit when they claim they care about those in our society who have disabilities, physical or mental. Someone broke the taboo and there is no excuse for it as far as these people are concerned, unless the person breaking the taboo is black. The perennially offended have NO standards. Grace Randolph is claiming that Tourette’s is no excuse because he must have thought that word before he said it. It is an excuse , because there is nobody in western society who doesn’t know that it’s inappropriate to say the N word in pretty much any situation, especially in the presence of a black person, but Tourette’s makes you say the most inappropriate thing in any situation. These people will lord this film about the very man who did this, as a film that highlights someone afflicted with a mental illness, and condemn the man it is about when they see his mental illness manifest in real life. They are unbelievable virtue signallers with double standards.
